meta.title: Use complex techniques to develop an electronics outcome
meta.purpose: This achievement standard involves using complex techniques to develop an electronics outcome.

grade_descriptors.N1: Evidence does not reach the minimum threshold for Achieved-level indicators.
grade_descriptors.N2: Some Achieved-level indicators are met, but not enough to satisfy all required Achieved criteria.
grade_descriptors.A3: All Achieved-level indicators are met; no Merit-level indicator meets the Merit threshold.
grade_descriptors.A4: All Achieved indicators are met and at least one Merit indicator is met, but overall performance is below the Merit threshold.
grade_descriptors.M5: All Achieved indicators are met and the Merit threshold is reached; no Excellence-level indicator meets the Excellence threshold.
grade_descriptors.M6: All Achieved indicators are met, all Merit indicators are met, and at least one Excellence indicator is met, but overall performance is below the Excellence threshold.
grade_descriptors.E7: All Achieved and Merit indicators are met and the Excellence threshold is reached, but not all Excellence indicators are met.
grade_descriptors.E8: All Achieved, Merit, and Excellence indicators are met.

omis.description: Shows a functional electronics outcome using appropriate resources and techniques.

positive_signals: Screenshots or videos showing the circuit operating as intended
Direct demonstration of the electronics outcome in action
Description of successful build and operation with evidence
Confirmation of full functionality through multiple tests
negative_signals: Only describing the build without any visual or operational evidence
Screenshots of code or schematics without showing operation
Incomplete or non-functional demonstrations
evidence_guide.common_confusion: A working build must be physically demonstrated, not just described in text.

success_examples: using appropriate resources and techniques to develop a functional electronics outcome
omis.source_ref: AS PDF p2, Explanatory Note 2 • Achieved bullet 1

omis.description: Constructs, tests, and analyses functional circuits to ensure performance to specifications.

positive_signals: Measurement of signal range, timing, or stability against specifications
Analysis of test results showing performance metrics
Comparison of actual vs. expected behavior with data
Documentation of testing procedures and outcomes
negative_signals: Vague statements like 'it worked fine' without specific data
Testing without reference to performance criteria
Lack of analysis or interpretation of test results
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Testing must include quantitative analysis against specifications, not just qualitative descriptions.

success_examples: constructing, testing, and analysing functional circuits to ensure that the electronics outcome performs to specifications
tested filtering and noise suppression to meet performance specifications
omis.source_ref: AS PDF p2, Explanatory Note 2 • Achieved bullet 2

omis.description: Provides evidence of testing, modifying, and debugging the outcome.

positive_signals: Description of identifying and correcting errors in code or wiring
Evidence of modifying components based on test results
Documentation of debugging processes like fixing logic conflicts
Multiple iterations of testing and adjustments
negative_signals: Only mentioning testing without showing modifications or debugging
Describing errors without explaining how they were fixed
Lack of evidence for all three processes: testing, modifying, debugging
evidence_guide.common_confusion: All three elements—testing, modification, and debugging—must be explicitly evidenced, not just implied.

success_examples: testing, modifying, debugging the outcome
used software interrupts to debug sensor feedback
omis.source_ref: AS PDF p2, Explanatory Note 2 • Achieved bullet 3

omis.description: Explains the behaviour and function of the electronics outcome.

positive_signals: Description of how components interact to achieve function
Explanation of subsystem interactions and outcomes
Clear linkage between parts and overall behavior
Technical details on signal flow or control logic
negative_signals: Only naming components without explaining interactions
Simple descriptions of appearance or basic features
Lack of detail on how parts contribute to function
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Explanation must go beyond naming parts to describe how they interact and produce the desired behavior.

success_examples: explaining the behaviour and function of the electronics outcome
explained how sensor input and microcontroller output interact to control the display
omis.source_ref: AS PDF p2, Explanatory Note 2 • Achieved bullet 4

omis.description: Explains the relevant communication protocol(s) used in the outcome.

positive_signals: Description of how data is transmitted between devices
Explanation of the role of each device in the protocol
Rationale for why the chosen protocol was appropriate
Details on protocol specifics like I2C, serial, or wireless
negative_signals: Merely naming the protocol without explanation
Lack of detail on data transmission or device roles
No justification for the protocol choice
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Simply stating the protocol name is not enough; explanation of its operation and appropriateness is required.

success_examples: explaining relevant communication protocols
explained how the I2C protocol transfers data between the MCU and sensor
omis.source_ref: AS PDF p2, Explanatory Note 2 • Achieved bullet 5; Commentary Achieved examples (I2C usage)

omis.description: Shows how at least two relevant implications are addressed in the outcome.

positive_signals: Explicit links between design decisions and implications like safety or usability
Description of how implications were met through specific choices
Examples of addressing functionality, aesthetics, or end-user needs
Evidence of considering at least two distinct implications
negative_signals: Listing implications without explaining how they were addressed
Vague references without specific design links
Focusing on only one implication
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Implications must be explicitly tied to design decisions, not just mentioned in isolation.

success_examples: addressing relevant implications
demonstrated how design choices improved usability and met end-user considerations
omis.source_ref: AS PDF p2, Explanatory Note 2 • Achieved bullet 6; AS PDF p2–3, Explanatory Note 4 (implications list)

omis.description: Demonstrates at least two complex techniques in the electronics outcome.

positive_signals: Implementation of communication protocols like I2C or wireless
Use of feedback control loops or interrupts
CAD/PCB fabrication or noise suppression filtering
Evidence of two distinct complex techniques in action
negative_signals: Mentioning difficulty without showing the process
Only one complex technique demonstrated
Describing techniques without evidence of implementation
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Evidence must show the actual use of techniques, not just their mention or perceived complexity.

success_examples: They have used at least two complex techniques
implemented I2C communication and feedback control loop
omis.source_ref: Moderator commentary (Achieved); AS PDF p2, Explanatory Note 3 (examples)

omis.description: Uses information from testing and analysis to ensure the circuit(s) functions reliably.

positive_signals: Repeated trials or data logs showing improved reliability
Measurement comparisons demonstrating consistency
Use of test data to make adjustments for reliability
Analysis of results leading to reliability enhancements
negative_signals: Simply stating that it 'works' without data
Testing without analysis or application to reliability
Lack of evidence from multiple tests
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Reliability must be evidenced through repeated testing and data analysis, not just a single successful run.

success_examples: using information from testing and analysis to ensure the circuit(s) functions reliably
averaged sensor values and checked against invalid inputs to improve consistency
omis.source_ref: AS PDF p2, Explanatory Note 2 • Merit definition

omis.description: Demonstrates that the system functions consistently in its intended location or context.

positive_signals: Consistent readings in the intended environment
Stability over time or under varying conditions
Testing in context showing reliable operation
Evidence of performance in real-world scenarios
negative_signals: A single successful demonstration without context
Testing in controlled conditions not matching intended use
Lack of evidence for consistency over time
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Consistency must be shown in the actual intended location or context, not just in ideal conditions.

success_examples: showed that the system can function in a consistent manner in its intended location
tested the system under varying conditions to confirm stable operation

omis.description: Undertakes iterative improvement throughout design, development, and testing, showing deliberate cycles and resulting refinements.

positive_signals: Multiple design-test-improve cycles documented
Refinements to circuit layout, code, or EMI performance
Evidence of deliberate iterations with reflections
Progressive improvements shown over time
negative_signals: A single revision without showing the cycle
Mentioning improvements without documenting the process
Lack of reflection on why changes were made
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Evidence must show multiple cycles of improvement, not just one change or final result.

success_examples: undertaking iterative improvement throughout the design, development and testing process
iteratively refined PCB layout and code to improve reliability
omis.source_ref: AS PDF p2, Explanatory Note 2 • Excellence bullet 1; Moderator commentary (Excellence)

omis.description: Justifies the choice of communication protocol(s) against alternatives, with advantages and disadvantages.

positive_signals: Comparison of alternative protocols (e.g., I2C vs serial)
Explanation of advantages and disadvantages of chosen protocol
Rationale for why it suits performance or reliability
Contrast with other options and justification
negative_signals: Listing advantages without comparing alternatives
Choosing a protocol without justification
Lack of discussion on disadvantages or alternatives
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Justification requires comparing alternatives, not just stating why the chosen one is good.

success_examples: justifying the choice of communication protocols
explained the advantages and disadvantages of using RS232 over other protocols
omis.source_ref: AS PDF p2, Explanatory Note 2 • Excellence bullet 2; Moderator commentary (Excellence)

omis.description: Justifies the selection of components and subsystems by comparing alternatives and explaining reasoning.

positive_signals: Rationale based on performance, efficiency, or requirements
Comparison of competing components or interfaces
Explanation of why chosen over alternatives
Links to design needs or performance criteria
negative_signals: Listing components without explaining choices
Choosing without comparison to alternatives
Lack of reasoning tied to performance or efficiency
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Justification must include why alternatives were not chosen, not just why the selected ones are suitable.

success_examples: justifying the choice of components and subsystems
compared competing components and interfaces for the same purpose and justified their decisions
omis.source_ref: AS PDF p2, Explanatory Note 2 • Excellence bullet 3; Moderator commentary (Excellence)
